statements.Name: TwoComponentsNotConnected.Statements.ConnectedRelation.IsConnectedRelation
statements.InlineTexReference: A reachability relation is connected if every pair of vertices is reachable.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def IsConnectedRelation {V : Type u} (G : SimpleGraph V) : Prop :=
  ∀ u v : V, G.Reachable u v

statements.Name: TwoComponentsNotConnected.Statements.TwoComponentsDisconnected.two_components_not_connected
statements.InlineTexReference: Suppose there is a nonempty left class of vertices and a nonempty right class of vertices. If no vertex in the left class can reach any vertex in the right class, then the reachability relation fails to connect all vertices. Therefore the relation is not connected.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
axiom two_components_not_connected {V : Type u} (G : SimpleGraph V)
    (left right : V → Prop) (leftVertex rightVertex : V)
    (left_mem : left leftVertex)
    (right_mem : right rightVertex)
    (separated : ∀ u v : V, left u → right v → ¬ G.Reachable u v) :
    ¬ IsConnectedRelation G

statements.Name: GraphCutVertex.Statements.CutVertexRemoval.cut_vertex_removal_not_connected
statements.Title: Cut Vertex Removal Is Disconnected
statements.InlineTexReference:
If deleting a vertex leaves two nonempty vertex classes with no reachable path between
the classes in the remaining graph, then the vertex-deleted graph is not connected.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
axiom cut_vertex_removal_not_connected {V : Type u} (G : SimpleGraph V) (cutVertex : V)
    (left right : ((⊤ : G.Subgraph).deleteVerts {cutVertex}).verts → Prop)
    (leftVertex rightVertex : ((⊤ : G.Subgraph).deleteVerts {cutVertex}).verts)
    (left_mem : left leftVertex)
    (right_mem : right rightVertex)
    (separated_after_removal :
      ∀ u v : ((⊤ : G.Subgraph).deleteVerts {cutVertex}).verts,
        left u → right v → ¬ ((⊤ : G.Subgraph).deleteVerts {cutVertex}).coe.Reachable u v) :
    ¬ ∀ u v : ((⊤ : G.Subgraph).deleteVerts {cutVertex}).verts,
      ((⊤ : G.Subgraph).deleteVerts {cutVertex}).coe.Reachable u v

proofs.Name: GraphCutVertex.Proofs.CutVertexRemoval.cut_vertex_removal_not_connected
proofs.Title: Cut Vertex Removal From Two Separated Classes

statements.Name: FragileMainResult.Statements.Main.KColorable
statements.Title: m-colorability
statements.InlineTexReference:
The statement package records the graph-theoretic vocabulary used in the
formalization of the main result.  A graph is \(m\)-colorable when it has a
mathlib coloring by the finite palette \(\mathrm{Fin}\,m\).  A set of vertices
is a separator when deleting it leaves two vertices in different connected
components, and a graph is three-connected when it has at least four vertices
and no separator of size at most two.

An \(m\)-fragile graph is one whose three-connected subgraphs are all
\((m-1)\)-colorable.  The predicate \(\mathrm{T3Conditions}\) is the conjunction
of the four coloring-extension conclusions C1--C4 from Theorem 3 of the paper.

The public axioms state Theorem 3, its direct colorability corollary, Theorem 2
in both contrapositive and chromatic-number form, and the final corollary that
graphs with no three-connected subgraph are four-colorable.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def KColorable (m : Nat) (G : SimpleGraph V) : Prop :=
  G.Colorable m

statements.Name: FragileMainResult.Statements.Main.deleteVertices
statements.Title: Deleted-vertex graph
statements.InlineTexReference:
The statement package records the graph-theoretic vocabulary used in the
formalization of the main result.  A graph is \(m\)-colorable when it has a
mathlib coloring by the finite palette \(\mathrm{Fin}\,m\).  A set of vertices
is a separator when deleting it leaves two vertices in different connected
components, and a graph is three-connected when it has at least four vertices
and no separator of size at most two.

An \(m\)-fragile graph is one whose three-connected subgraphs are all
\((m-1)\)-colorable.  The predicate \(\mathrm{T3Conditions}\) is the conjunction
of the four coloring-extension conclusions C1--C4 from Theorem 3 of the paper.

The public axioms state Theorem 3, its direct colorability corollary, Theorem 2
in both contrapositive and chromatic-number form, and the final corollary that
graphs with no three-connected subgraph are four-colorable.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def deleteVertices (G : SimpleGraph V) (S : Finset V) : SimpleGraph {v : V // v ∉ S} :=
  G.induce {v : V | v ∉ S}

statements.Name: FragileMainResult.Statements.Main.IsVertexSeparator
statements.Title: Vertex separator
statements.InlineTexReference:
The statement package records the graph-theoretic vocabulary used in the
formalization of the main result.  A graph is \(m\)-colorable when it has a
mathlib coloring by the finite palette \(\mathrm{Fin}\,m\).  A set of vertices
is a separator when deleting it leaves two vertices in different connected
components, and a graph is three-connected when it has at least four vertices
and no separator of size at most two.

An \(m\)-fragile graph is one whose three-connected subgraphs are all
\((m-1)\)-colorable.  The predicate \(\mathrm{T3Conditions}\) is the conjunction
of the four coloring-extension conclusions C1--C4 from Theorem 3 of the paper.

The public axioms state Theorem 3, its direct colorability corollary, Theorem 2
in both contrapositive and chromatic-number form, and the final corollary that
graphs with no three-connected subgraph are four-colorable.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def IsVertexSeparator (G : SimpleGraph V) (S : Finset V) : Prop :=
  ∃ x y : {v : V // v ∉ S}, ¬ (deleteVertices G S).Reachable x y

statements.Name: FragileMainResult.Statements.Main.ThreeConnected
statements.Title: Three-connected graph
statements.InlineTexReference:
The statement package records the graph-theoretic vocabulary used in the
formalization of the main result.  A graph is \(m\)-colorable when it has a
mathlib coloring by the finite palette \(\mathrm{Fin}\,m\).  A set of vertices
is a separator when deleting it leaves two vertices in different connected
components, and a graph is three-connected when it has at least four vertices
and no separator of size at most two.

An \(m\)-fragile graph is one whose three-connected subgraphs are all
\((m-1)\)-colorable.  The predicate \(\mathrm{T3Conditions}\) is the conjunction
of the four coloring-extension conclusions C1--C4 from Theorem 3 of the paper.

The public axioms state Theorem 3, its direct colorability corollary, Theorem 2
in both contrapositive and chromatic-number form, and the final corollary that
graphs with no three-connected subgraph are four-colorable.

statements.Name: FragileMainResult.Statements.Main.MFragile
statements.Title: m-fragile graph
statements.InlineTexReference:
The statement package records the graph-theoretic vocabulary used in the
formalization of the main result.  A graph is \(m\)-colorable when it has a
mathlib coloring by the finite palette \(\mathrm{Fin}\,m\).  A set of vertices
is a separator when deleting it leaves two vertices in different connected
components, and a graph is three-connected when it has at least four vertices
and no separator of size at most two.

An \(m\)-fragile graph is one whose three-connected subgraphs are all
\((m-1)\)-colorable.  The predicate \(\mathrm{T3Conditions}\) is the conjunction
of the four coloring-extension conclusions C1--C4 from Theorem 3 of the paper.

The public axioms state Theorem 3, its direct colorability corollary, Theorem 2
in both contrapositive and chromatic-number form, and the final corollary that
graphs with no three-connected subgraph are four-colorable.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def MFragile (m : Nat) (G : SimpleGraph V) : Prop :=
  ∀ H : G.Subgraph, ThreeConnected H.coe → KColorable (m - 1) H.coe

statements.Name: FragileMainResult.Statements.Main.HasNoThreeConnectedSubgraph
statements.Title: Fragile graph
statements.InlineTexReference:
The statement package records the graph-theoretic vocabulary used in the
formalization of the main result.  A graph is \(m\)-colorable when it has a
mathlib coloring by the finite palette \(\mathrm{Fin}\,m\).  A set of vertices
is a separator when deleting it leaves two vertices in different connected
components, and a graph is three-connected when it has at least four vertices
and no separator of size at most two.

An \(m\)-fragile graph is one whose three-connected subgraphs are all
\((m-1)\)-colorable.  The predicate \(\mathrm{T3Conditions}\) is the conjunction
of the four coloring-extension conclusions C1--C4 from Theorem 3 of the paper.

The public axioms state Theorem 3, its direct colorability corollary, Theorem 2
in both contrapositive and chromatic-number form, and the final corollary that
graphs with no three-connected subgraph are four-colorable.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def HasNoThreeConnectedSubgraph (G : SimpleGraph V) : Prop :=
  ∀ H : G.Subgraph, ¬ ThreeConnected H.coe

statements.Name: FragileMainResult.Statements.Main.T3Conditions
statements.Title: The four extension conditions
statements.InlineTexReference:
The statement package records the graph-theoretic vocabulary used in the
formalization of the main result.  A graph is \(m\)-colorable when it has a
mathlib coloring by the finite palette \(\mathrm{Fin}\,m\).  A set of vertices
is a separator when deleting it leaves two vertices in different connected
components, and a graph is three-connected when it has at least four vertices
and no separator of size at most two.

An \(m\)-fragile graph is one whose three-connected subgraphs are all
\((m-1)\)-colorable.  The predicate \(\mathrm{T3Conditions}\) is the conjunction
of the four coloring-extension conclusions C1--C4 from Theorem 3 of the paper.

The public axioms state Theorem 3, its direct colorability corollary, Theorem 2
in both contrapositive and chromatic-number form, and the final corollary that
graphs with no three-connected subgraph are four-colorable.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def T3Conditions [DecidableEq V] (m : Nat) (G : SimpleGraph V) : Prop :=
  (∀ {x y : V}, ¬ G.Adj x y →
    ∃ c : G.Coloring (Fin m), c x = c y) ∧
  (∀ {x y : V}, x ≠ y →
    ∃ c : G.Coloring (Fin m), c x ≠ c y) ∧
  (∀ {x y z : V}, x ≠ y → x ≠ z → y ≠ z →
    ∃ c : G.Coloring (Fin m),
      c x ∉ ({c y, c z} : Finset (Fin m))) ∧
  (∀ {x y z : V}, x ≠ y → x ≠ z → y ≠ z →
    ¬ (G.Adj x y ∧ G.Adj x z ∧ G.Adj y z) →
    ∃ c : G.Coloring (Fin m),
      (({x, y, z} : Finset V).image fun w => c w).card = 2)

statements.Name: FragileMainResult.Statements.Main.theorem3_mfragile
statements.Title: Theorem 3
statements.InlineTexReference:
The statement package records the graph-theoretic vocabulary used in the
formalization of the main result.  A graph is \(m\)-colorable when it has a
mathlib coloring by the finite palette \(\mathrm{Fin}\,m\).  A set of vertices
is a separator when deleting it leaves two vertices in different connected
components, and a graph is three-connected when it has at least four vertices
and no separator of size at most two.

An \(m\)-fragile graph is one whose three-connected subgraphs are all
\((m-1)\)-colorable.  The predicate \(\mathrm{T3Conditions}\) is the conjunction
of the four coloring-extension conclusions C1--C4 from Theorem 3 of the paper.

The public axioms state Theorem 3, its direct colorability corollary, Theorem 2
in both contrapositive and chromatic-number form, and the final corollary that
graphs with no three-connected subgraph are four-colorable.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
axiom theorem3_mfragile {V : Type u} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
    (m : Nat) (G : SimpleGraph V)
    (hm : 4 ≤ m) (hfrag : MFragile m G) :
    T3Conditions m G

statements.Name: FragileMainResult.Statements.Main.mfragile_colorable
statements.Title: m-fragile graphs are m-colorable
statements.InlineTexReference:
The statement package records the graph-theoretic vocabulary used in the
formalization of the main result.  A graph is \(m\)-colorable when it has a
mathlib coloring by the finite palette \(\mathrm{Fin}\,m\).  A set of vertices
is a separator when deleting it leaves two vertices in different connected
components, and a graph is three-connected when it has at least four vertices
and no separator of size at most two.

An \(m\)-fragile graph is one whose three-connected subgraphs are all
\((m-1)\)-colorable.  The predicate \(\mathrm{T3Conditions}\) is the conjunction
of the four coloring-extension conclusions C1--C4 from Theorem 3 of the paper.

The public axioms state Theorem 3, its direct colorability corollary, Theorem 2
in both contrapositive and chromatic-number form, and the final corollary that
graphs with no three-connected subgraph are four-colorable.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
axiom mfragile_colorable {V : Type u} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
    (m : Nat) (G : SimpleGraph V)
    (hm : 4 ≤ m) (hfrag : MFragile m G) :
    KColorable m G

statements.Name: FragileMainResult.Statements.Main.theorem2_contra
statements.Title: Theorem 2, contrapositive form
statements.InlineTexReference:
The statement package records the graph-theoretic vocabulary used in the
formalization of the main result.  A graph is \(m\)-colorable when it has a
mathlib coloring by the finite palette \(\mathrm{Fin}\,m\).  A set of vertices
is a separator when deleting it leaves two vertices in different connected
components, and a graph is three-connected when it has at least four vertices
and no separator of size at most two.

An \(m\)-fragile graph is one whose three-connected subgraphs are all
\((m-1)\)-colorable.  The predicate \(\mathrm{T3Conditions}\) is the conjunction
of the four coloring-extension conclusions C1--C4 from Theorem 3 of the paper.

The public axioms state Theorem 3, its direct colorability corollary, Theorem 2
in both contrapositive and chromatic-number form, and the final corollary that
graphs with no three-connected subgraph are four-colorable.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
axiom theorem2_contra {V : Type u} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
    (m : Nat) (G : SimpleGraph V)
    (hm : 4 ≤ m) (hnot : ¬ KColorable m G) :
    ∃ H : G.Subgraph, ThreeConnected H.coe ∧ ¬ KColorable (m - 1) H.coe

statements.Name: FragileMainResult.Statements.Main.theorem2
statements.Title: Theorem 2
statements.InlineTexReference:
The statement package records the graph-theoretic vocabulary used in the
formalization of the main result.  A graph is \(m\)-colorable when it has a
mathlib coloring by the finite palette \(\mathrm{Fin}\,m\).  A set of vertices
is a separator when deleting it leaves two vertices in different connected
components, and a graph is three-connected when it has at least four vertices
and no separator of size at most two.

An \(m\)-fragile graph is one whose three-connected subgraphs are all
\((m-1)\)-colorable.  The predicate \(\mathrm{T3Conditions}\) is the conjunction
of the four coloring-extension conclusions C1--C4 from Theorem 3 of the paper.

The public axioms state Theorem 3, its direct colorability corollary, Theorem 2
in both contrapositive and chromatic-number form, and the final corollary that
graphs with no three-connected subgraph are four-colorable.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
axiom theorem2 {V : Type u} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
    (m : Nat) (G : SimpleGraph V)
    (hm : 4 ≤ m) (hchi : ((m + 1 : Nat) : ℕ∞) ≤ G.chromaticNumber) :
    ∃ H : G.Subgraph, ThreeConnected H.coe ∧ (m : ℕ∞) ≤ H.coe.chromaticNumber

statements.Name: FragileMainResult.Statements.Main.fragile_four_colorable
statements.Title: Fragile graphs are four-colorable
statements.InlineTexReference:
The statement package records the graph-theoretic vocabulary used in the
formalization of the main result.  A graph is \(m\)-colorable when it has a
mathlib coloring by the finite palette \(\mathrm{Fin}\,m\).  A set of vertices
is a separator when deleting it leaves two vertices in different connected
components, and a graph is three-connected when it has at least four vertices
and no separator of size at most two.

An \(m\)-fragile graph is one whose three-connected subgraphs are all
\((m-1)\)-colorable.  The predicate \(\mathrm{T3Conditions}\) is the conjunction
of the four coloring-extension conclusions C1--C4 from Theorem 3 of the paper.

The public axioms state Theorem 3, its direct colorability corollary, Theorem 2
in both contrapositive and chromatic-number form, and the final corollary that
graphs with no three-connected subgraph are four-colorable.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
axiom fragile_four_colorable {V : Type u} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
    (G : SimpleGraph V) (hfragile : HasNoThreeConnectedSubgraph G) :
    KColorable 4 G

proofs.Name: FragileMainResult.Proofs.Main.mfragile_colorable
proofs.Title: Proof that m-fragile graphs are m-colorable

proofs.Name: FragileMainResult.Proofs.Main.fragile_four_colorable
proofs.Title: Proof that fragile graphs are four-colorable

SubmissionData.SubmissionName: Functional Equivalence of Twin-Width and Mixed Minor Number

statements.Name: TwinWidthMixedMinorNumberEquivalence.Statements.Main.GraphParam
statements.Title: Graph parameter
statements.InlineTexReference: The public graph parameters are twin-width and mixed minor number.  The submitted directional statements say that twin-width is bounded by some numerical function of mixed minor number, and that mixed minor number is linearly bounded by twin-width.  The main statement is their functional equivalence.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def GraphParam :=
  ∀ {V : Type}, [Fintype V] → [DecidableEq V] → SimpleGraph V → Nat

statements.Name: TwinWidthMixedMinorNumberEquivalence.Statements.Main.FunctionallyEquivalent
statements.Title: Functional equivalence
statements.InlineTexReference: The public graph parameters are twin-width and mixed minor number.  The submitted directional statements say that twin-width is bounded by some numerical function of mixed minor number, and that mixed minor number is linearly bounded by twin-width.  The main statement is their functional equivalence.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def FunctionallyEquivalent (p q : GraphParam) : Prop :=
  (∃ f : Nat → Nat, ∀ {V : Type} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V] (G : SimpleGraph V),
    p (V := V) G ≤ f (q (V := V) G)) ∧
  (∃ g : Nat → Nat, ∀ {V : Type} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V] (G : SimpleGraph V),
    q (V := V) G ≤ g (p (V := V) G))

statements.Name: TwinWidthMixedMinorNumberEquivalence.Statements.Main.twinWidth
statements.Title: Twin-width graph parameter
statements.InlineTexReference: The public graph parameters are twin-width and mixed minor number.  The submitted directional statements say that twin-width is bounded by some numerical function of mixed minor number, and that mixed minor number is linearly bounded by twin-width.  The main statement is their functional equivalence.
statements.InlineLeanStatement: axiom twinWidth : GraphParam

statements.Name: TwinWidthMixedMinorNumberEquivalence.Statements.Main.mixedMinorNumber
statements.Title: Mixed minor number graph parameter
statements.InlineTexReference: The public graph parameters are twin-width and mixed minor number.  The submitted directional statements say that twin-width is bounded by some numerical function of mixed minor number, and that mixed minor number is linearly bounded by twin-width.  The main statement is their functional equivalence.
statements.InlineLeanStatement: axiom mixedMinorNumber : GraphParam

statements.Name: TwinWidthMixedMinorNumberEquivalence.Statements.Main.twin_width_bounded_by_mixed_minor_number
statements.Title: Twin-width is bounded by mixed minor number
statements.InlineTexReference: The public graph parameters are twin-width and mixed minor number.  The submitted directional statements say that twin-width is bounded by some numerical function of mixed minor number, and that mixed minor number is linearly bounded by twin-width.  The main statement is their functional equivalence.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
axiom twin_width_bounded_by_mixed_minor_number :
    ∃ f : Nat → Nat, ∀ {V : Type} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V] (G : SimpleGraph V),
      twinWidth (V := V) G ≤ f (mixedMinorNumber (V := V) G)

statements.Name: TwinWidthMixedMinorNumberEquivalence.Statements.Main.mixed_minor_number_le_twin_width_linear
statements.Title: Mixed minor number is linearly bounded by twin-width
statements.InlineTexReference: The public graph parameters are twin-width and mixed minor number.  The submitted directional statements say that twin-width is bounded by some numerical function of mixed minor number, and that mixed minor number is linearly bounded by twin-width.  The main statement is their functional equivalence.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
axiom mixed_minor_number_le_twin_width_linear
    {V : Type} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V] (G : SimpleGraph V) :
    mixedMinorNumber (V := V) G ≤ 2 * (twinWidth (V := V) G + 3) + 2

statements.Name: TwinWidthMixedMinorNumberEquivalence.Statements.Main.twin_width_functionally_equivalent_mixed_minor_number
statements.Title: Twin-width and mixed minor number are functionally equivalent
statements.InlineTexReference: The public graph parameters are twin-width and mixed minor number.  The submitted directional statements say that twin-width is bounded by some numerical function of mixed minor number, and that mixed minor number is linearly bounded by twin-width.  The main statement is their functional equivalence.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
axiom twin_width_functionally_equivalent_mixed_minor_number :
    FunctionallyEquivalent twinWidth mixedMinorNumber

proofs.Name: TwinWidthMixedMinorNumberEquivalence.Proofs.Main.twin_width_functionally_equivalent_mixed_minor_number
proofs.Title: Proof of functional equivalence from the two directional bounds

SubmissionData.SubmissionName: Twin-Width Can Be Exponential in Treewidth

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponential.Statements.Main.GraphParam
statements.Title: Graph parameter
statements.InlineLeanStatement: def GraphParam := TwinWidthMixedMinorNumberEquivalence.Statements.Main.GraphParam

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponential.Statements.Main.twinWidth
statements.Title: Twin-width graph parameter imported from the mixed-minor-number submission
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
noncomputable def twinWidth : GraphParam :=
  TwinWidthMixedMinorNumberEquivalence.Statements.Main.twinWidth

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponential.Statements.Main.treewidth
statements.Title: Treewidth graph parameter
statements.InlineLeanStatement: axiom treewidth : GraphParam

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponential.Statements.Main.bonnet_depres_exponential_gap
statements.Title: Bonnet-Depres exponential twin-width gap
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
axiom bonnet_depres_exponential_gap
    (k : Nat) :
    ∃ (V : Type) (_ : Fintype V) (_ : DecidableEq V)
      (G : SimpleGraph V),
      treewidth (V := V) G ≤ 2 * k + 4 ∧ 2 ^ k < twinWidth (V := V) G

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponential.Statements.Main.twin_width_can_be_exponential_in_treewidth
statements.Title: Twin-width can be exponential in treewidth
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
axiom twin_width_can_be_exponential_in_treewidth
    (k : Nat) :
    ∃ (V : Type) (_ : Fintype V) (_ : DecidableEq V)
      (G : SimpleGraph V),
      treewidth (V := V) G ≤ 2 * k + 4 ∧ 2 ^ k < twinWidth (V := V) G

proofs.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponential.Proofs.Main.twin_width_can_be_exponential_in_treewidth
proofs.Title: Proof of the final exponential gap from the Bonnet-Depres gap statement
